era_context: Late 15th century: Age of Exploration in full momentum. The Iberian Peninsula consolidates Christian kingdoms (Granada falls to Catholic Monarchs in 1492); Portuguese maritime expansion reaches India's coast (Vasco da Gama, 1498); Ottoman Empire dominates Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans under Bayezid II; Ming Dynasty rules China under Hongwu's successors; Aztec Empire at zenith under Ahuitzotl in Mesoamerica; sub-Saharan African kingdoms (Kongo, Mali successor states, Great Zimbabwe) engage in expanding Atlantic slave trade.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited European documentation; oral traditions and archaeological evidence underrepresented)
Interior Southeast Asia and Pacific island societies
North American indigenous polities (sparse European contact records)

events.title: Granada War concludes; Catholic Monarchs besiege final Nasrid stronghold
events.description: The Reconquista reaches its climax as Ferdinand and Isabella's forces intensify siege operations against the last independent Islamic state in Iberia. The Nasrid Emirate of Granada, ruled by Muhammad XII (Boabdil), faces overwhelming Christian pressure. This year marks the penultimate phase before the city's surrender in January 1492. The campaign consolidates Christian territorial dominance and presages the expulsion of Muslims and Jews from Iberia.

sources.name: Andrés Bernáldez, Historia de los Reyes Católicos (chronicle compiled early 16th century, based on contemporary observation)
Pulgar, Crónica de los Reyes Católicos

events.certainty_note: The Granada War's timeline is well-documented in multiple near-contemporary Spanish chronicles and later historiographic consensus. Siege operations in 1491 are attested.

events.title: Portuguese maritime expedition reaches Congo River mouth; first European contact with Kingdom of Kongo
events.description: Portuguese navigator Diogo Cão (or his successor) makes contact with the Kingdom of Kongo, establishing the first documented European-Kongo diplomatic exchange. The Portuguese crown sends emissaries and receives Kongo ambassadors in return. This encounter initiates the long, complex relationship between Portugal and Kongo, beginning with trade and religious missions, later devolving into the Atlantic slave trade. The event marks a pivotal moment in African-European contact and the beginning of Kongo's integration into Atlantic commerce.
key_figures: João I (Nzinga a Nkuwu), King of Kongo

sources.name: Rui de Pina, Crónica de El-Rei D. João II (Portuguese royal chronicle, early 16th century)
Duarte Pacheco Pereira, Esmeraldo de Situ Orbis (Portuguese geographical account, early 16th century)
Kongo oral traditions and later Portuguese missionary accounts

events.certainty_note: The exact year of first Portuguese-Kongo contact is debated. Diogo Cão's voyage is dated variously to 1482–1486; subsequent contact and embassy exchange may have occurred in 1491 or nearby years. Historiographic consensus places formal diplomatic contact in the early 1490s.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet construction accelerates under Yongle Emperor; Ming naval dominance consolidated
events.description: The Ming Dynasty continues construction and preparation of the massive treasure ships (baochuan) for Zheng He's voyages of exploration. By 1491, the shipyards at Nanjing are producing vessels of unprecedented scale—some estimates suggest 400+ feet in length. These ships represent the apex of 15th-century maritime technology and reflect Ming imperial ambitions to project power across the Indian Ocean. The fleet's construction demonstrates Ming technological sophistication and centralized state capacity.

sources.name: Luo Maodeng, Zheng He xia xiyang ji (Ming-era account of Zheng He's voyages, compiled early 17th century)

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's voyages are well-documented in Ming records. The exact timeline of ship construction is less precise; 1491 falls within the period of active preparation for the voyages (which began 1405). Historiographic consensus places major construction in the early 15th century, with ongoing maintenance and preparation through the 1490s.

events.title: Ahuitzotl consolidates Aztec territorial expansion; military campaigns in southern Mesoamerica
events.description: Ahuitzotl, the eighth Aztec emperor (r. 1486–1502), continues aggressive military expansion and consolidation of tributary networks. By 1491, the Aztec Empire has reached its territorial zenith, controlling much of central Mexico and extending influence into the Yucatan and southern regions. Ahuitzotl's reign is marked by extensive military campaigns, temple construction (including the expansion of the Templo Mayor), and the integration of conquered peoples into the tribute system. This year falls within the period of maximum Aztec imperial power before Spanish contact.
key_figures: Tlacaelel (Aztec advisor, died 1487, but his policies continued)

sources.name: Codex Mendoza (Aztec pictorial manuscript, compiled post-conquest but based on pre-conquest records)
Sahagún, Florentine Codex (Spanish friar's compilation of Nahua accounts, 16th century)
Durán, Historia de las Indias de Nueva España (Spanish chronicler, 16th century)

events.certainty_note: Ahuitzotl's reign and military campaigns are well-attested in Aztec codices and Spanish colonial-era accounts. Specific events in 1491 are less precisely dated; the year falls within his documented period of expansion.

events.title: Ottoman-Venetian War (Fourth Venetian-Ottoman War) continues; naval and territorial conflicts in Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: The Fourth Venetian-Ottoman War (1499–1503) is in progress, though 1491 falls before its formal declaration. Ottoman forces under Bayezid II are consolidating control of the Balkans and Eastern Mediterranean, while Venice defends its remaining colonial possessions and trade routes. The conflict reflects the broader shift in Mediterranean power from Venice to the Ottoman Empire. By 1491, Ottoman naval and military superiority is becoming evident, presaging Venice's loss of key territories in the coming years.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and Turkish chronicles (e.g., Tursun Beg, Sakaname)
Venetian state archives and chronicles (e.g., Marino Sanudo, Diaries)

events.certainty_note: The Fourth Venetian-Ottoman War is well-documented. However, 1491 is before the war's formal declaration (1499). Ottoman-Venetian tensions and skirmishes in 1491 are attested but less precisely dated than the formal war period.

events.title: Portuguese crown consolidates Atlantic island colonization; sugar production expands in Madeira and Canary Islands
events.description: Portuguese colonial efforts in the Atlantic islands (Madeira, Azores, Cape Verde) are expanding sugar cultivation and establishing plantation economies. By 1491, Madeira has become a major sugar producer, using enslaved African labor imported via Portuguese traders. This development presages the later transatlantic slave trade and the plantation model that will dominate colonial economies. The islands serve as a testing ground for colonial administration, labor systems, and agricultural export models.

sources.name: Portuguese royal charters and commercial records (Arquivo Nacional da Torre do Tombo)
Zurara, Crónica da Guiné (Portuguese chronicle, 15th century)

events.certainty_note: Portuguese colonization of Atlantic islands and sugar production are well-documented in Portuguese archives and contemporary accounts. The timeline of expansion is clear.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 1491 as a pivotal year is partly retrospective. While Granada's siege and Portuguese exploration are well-attested, the year itself is not marked by singular transformative events in most regions. The 'Age of Exploration' narrative privileges European perspectives; from the viewpoint of Ming China, Ottoman Empire, or Aztec Empire, 1491 represents continuity rather than rupture. Modern scholarship has questioned the 'discovery' framing of European exploration, emphasizing instead the agency and sophistication of non-European societies. Additionally, the dating of Portuguese-Kongo contact to 1491 is approximate; some sources place it earlier (1482–1486) or later (1490s).
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1491 is rich for European, Ottoman, and Ming sources, reflecting the survival of court records, chronicles, and administrative documents. African, Mesoamerican, and indigenous sources are mediated through later European compilation or oral tradition, introducing bias toward European perspectives and interests. The historical record privileges political and military events over economic, social, or environmental changes. Gaps exist in the documentation of daily life, demographic trends, and the perspectives of non-elite populations. The year's significance is partly constructed by later historians emphasizing the prelude to 1492 (Columbus's voyage) and the 'Age of Exploration,' which may overstate the year's actual historical weight.

graph_edges.note: Granada siege concludes with surrender in January 1492
Portuguese-Kongo contact leads to formal embassy exchange
Zheng He's voyages represent continuation of Ming maritime expansion
Ahuitzotl's expansion sets stage for Montezuma II's reign and Spanish contact
Ottoman-Venetian tensions escalate into formal war in 1499
Atlantic island colonization presages transatlantic slave trade
